n: Musée de la Chasse et de la Nature
hook: A hunting museum that doubles as a cabinet of surreal taxidermy
facts: Set in the Hotel de Guenegaud (1651-55), the only surviving private mansion by architect Francois Mansart.
Founded 1964 by industrialist Francois Sommer and his wife Jacqueline around their personal collection.
Themed rooms blend antique guns, tapestries and stuffed beasts with contemporary art.
w: Not your standard trophy room. Behind a Mansart facade, panelled salons pile up muskets, falconry hoods and a wide-eyed polar bear, then slip in contemporary art so the join blurs. A whole ceiling of owls watches you. Hunting, nature and taste, deadpan and strange. Glorious oddity in the Marais.

hook: A green-shuttered villa where George Sand's salon still lingers
facts: Housed in the 1830s home and studio of Dutch-born painter Ary Scheffer at the foot of Montmartre.
Scheffer hosted Friday salons drawing Chopin, Delacroix, Liszt, Rossini and neighbour George Sand.
Became a city museum in 1987; rooms hold Sand relics and jewellery.
w: Down a cobbled alley off the old Nouvelle Athenes, a shuttered villa keeps the ghost of a salon. Painter Ary Scheffer drew Chopin, Liszt and his neighbour George Sand to Friday evenings here. Now her jewels and plaster casts sit in rose-tangled rooms with a tea garden. Romanticism, preserved in aspic.

hook: The novelist's bolt-hole, with a back door for dodging creditors
facts: The only surviving Paris home of Honore de Balzac, who lodged here 1840-47 under a false name.
Here he revised 'La Comedie Humaine'; his desk and coffee pot are on show.
A second exit onto rue Berton let him slip away from debt collectors.
legend: They say Balzac fuelled marathon writing nights on fifty cups of coffee a day.
w: Balzac holed up in this Passy cottage under a fake name, dodging creditors with a back door onto rue Berton. Between coffee binges he hacked away at 'La Comedie Humaine' at the little desk still here. The garden looks out toward the Eiffel Tower he never saw. A writer's hideout, debts and all.

hook: A fortified medieval tower a murderous duke built to sleep safe
facts: Raised 1409-11 by John the Fearless, Duke of Burgundy, who had ordered the killing of Louis of Orleans.
Last vestige of the vanished Hotel de Bourgogne residence.
Its stone vault carves oak, hawthorn and hops, emblems of the Burgundian house.
w: John the Fearless had a royal cousin murdered, then bolted this stone keep onto his mansion so he could sleep behind thick walls. Now stranded in a school courtyard, it climbs past a flamboyant vault carved with oak and hops, Burgundian heraldry frozen mid-feud. One of the city's last true medieval rooms.

hook: A sculptor's atelier left as a forest of plaster giants
facts: The studio of Antoine Bourdelle (1861-1929), pupil of Rodin, kept from 1885 until his death.
Garden and halls hold monumental bronzes and plaster casts amid the original workshops.
Entry to the permanent collection is free.
w: Rodin's old assistant worked here for forty-odd years, and they never cleared the place out. Cavernous studios and a green courtyard brim with monumental plasters and bronzes, the dust and skylights intact. Heracles draws his bow over a quiet Montparnasse lane. A working atelier, simply paused. And free.

hook: A banker-revolutionary's Asian hoard beside Parc Monceau
facts: Founded 1898 in the mansion of Italian-born financier Henri Cernuschi (1821-1896).
Second-oldest Asian art museum in France, with some 15,000 objects.
Centrepiece is a monumental Japanese Buddha brought back from Cernuschi's travels.
w: A firebrand of the 1848 revolution turned banker, Cernuschi sailed to Japan and shipped home a giant Meguro Buddha, then built a mansion to hold it. The result, gifted to the city, crams bronzes and jades from China to Korea beside Parc Monceau. Free, second-oldest of its kind, gloriously overlooked.

hook: A glass-roofed 1820s arcade paved in mosaic splendour
facts: Built 1823, opened 1826, on plans by architect Francois-Jacques Delannoy.
Neo-classical decor with mosaic floors laid by Italian craftsman Giandomenico Facchina.
Listed as a historic monument since 1974; the master-criminal Vidocq once lived nearby.
w: Step off the street into 1826: a glass canopy, swirling Facchina mosaics underfoot and goddesses of commerce moulded overhead. Built to lure shoppers between the Palais-Royal and the Bourse, it survived the Haussmann wrecking ball that flattened its rivals. Faded grandeur with a tearoom. Pure nineteenth-century theatre.

hook: The oldest covered passage, born of painted panoramas and gaslight
facts: Opened 1799, the oldest of the surviving Paris covered passages.
Named for two rotundas that once showed vast 360-degree painted panoramas at its entrance.
Among the first public spaces in Paris lit by gas, around 1816-17.
w: The grandfather of all the arcades, open since 1799 and named for the giant painted panoramas that once drew crowds at its mouth. Early gaslight glittered here; now it is engravers, stamp dealers and crowded bistros under a battered glass roof. Zola set a scene in it. Shabby, alive, the original template.

hook: The city's oldest covered market, named for orphans in red
facts: Trading since 1615, the oldest covered market in Paris.
Named for a nearby orphanage whose children wore red, the colour of charity.
Listed a historic monument in 1982; now a warren of produce stalls and food counters.
w: Open since 1615 and named for an orphanage whose foundlings wore charity red, the city's oldest covered market nearly fell to demolition in the 1990s before locals saved it. Now greengrocers and cheesemongers share the iron sheds with Moroccan, Japanese and Lebanese cooks. Lunch elbow-to-elbow, four centuries deep.

hook: A Cubist sculptor's cottage and garden hidden off rue d'Assas
facts: Home and studio of Russian-born sculptor Ossip Zadkine (1890-1967) from 1928 until his death.
Tucked in a leafy plot between the Luxembourg Gardens and Montparnasse.
Permanent collection of his angular wood and stone figures is free to enter.
w: Behind a wall on rue d'Assas, Ossip Zadkine found a scrap of countryside and stayed forty years. His angular wood and stone figures still loiter in the garden and low studios, ivy creeping over the bronze. Few find the gate. A pocket of Ecole de Paris calm, free, two steps from the Luxembourg.

hook: A royal furniture store turned palace on Place de la Concorde
facts: Built 1757-74 by Ange-Jacques Gabriel as the royal Garde-Meuble, storing the crown's furnishings.
Served as the Navy ministry from the Revolution until 2015, then opened as a museum in 2021.
The crown jewels were sensationally stolen from here in 1792.
w: Built to warehouse the king's furniture, this colonnaded palace watched the guillotine work the square below, then housed the Navy for two centuries. Thieves looted the crown jewels from it in 1792. Reopened in 2021, its restored apartments and gilded salons gleam again over Concorde. Faded grandeur, lavishly revived.

hook: A magic museum in vaulted cellars under Sade's old house
facts: Set in 16th-century stone cellars beneath a building once linked to the Marquis de Sade.
Collection runs from distorting mirrors and trick boxes to optical illusions.
Adjoins a museum of more than a hundred automata; live conjuring shows run on opening days.
w: Down stone steps into vaulted Marais cellars, said to sit beneath a house once tied to the Marquis de Sade, lurks a temple of illusion. Distorting mirrors, trick chests, automata and a live conjuror who makes things vanish under the low arches. Children shriek; adults squint. Sleight of hand, underground.

hook: A 1920s brick cul-de-sac that thinks it's in London
facts: Laid out 1926 off Avenue Junot on the slopes of Montmartre.
Lined with English-style brick cottages, slate roofs and private gardens.
Renamed in 1936 for caricaturist Charles Leandre; a windmill stood here before.
w: Turn off Avenue Junot and Paris dissolves: a quiet 1926 lane of red-brick cottages, slate roofs and front gardens that could pass for Hampstead. A windmill once ground flour on the spot. Cabaret folk and artists moved in; now it dozes, mock-Tudor and improbable, just above the Montmartre crowds. Eccentric and serene.

hook: A revolutionary alley where the guillotine was first tested
facts: At number 9 a carpenter's workshop saw early guillotine trials, reputedly on sheep.
Marat printed his paper 'L'Ami du Peuple' at number 8 before his 1793 murder.
A surviving stretch of Philip Augustus's medieval wall stands in a side courtyard.
legend: They say the machine was rehearsed here on straw and live sheep before its grim debut.
w: A cobbled slot of revolution. At number 9 they tested an early guillotine; at number 8 Marat ran off 'L'Ami du Peuple' before Charlotte Corday's knife found him. A tower of Philip Augustus's old wall still juts in a courtyard. Cafe terraces now fill the lane that midwifed the Terror. History at knee height.

hook: A willow-tipped prow of the Cite where Templars burned
facts: A triangular garden at the downstream tip of the Ile de la Cite, by the Pont Neuf.
Created 1884 from former islets, among them the Ile aux Juifs.
Nicknamed for Henri IV, the 'green gallant' whose statue rears above on the bridge.
legend: Legend holds the last Templar grand master, Jacques de Molay, was burned at the stake on the islet now buried under this lawn.
w: The pointed prow of the old island, a lawn and a weeping willow at water level below the Pont Neuf. Legend puts the burning of the last Templar master, Jacques de Molay, on the islet now under this turf. Named for amorous old Henri IV up on his horse. The Seine slides past both elbows. Quiet, low, storied.

hook: A tiny museum of every language, crammed down a back lane
facts: Opened 2013, founded by New Zealand linguist Mark Oremland.
Hands-on exhibits cover phonetics, sign language, code, writing systems and Esperanto.
Tucked on cobbled rue Servandoni between the Luxembourg and Saint-Sulpice.
w: One man's obsession with how humans talk, squeezed into a cellar off rue Servandoni. Touch-and-listen displays roam from clicking consonants to sign language, Morse, hieroglyphs and Esperanto, an Enigma machine sitting in the corner. Small, nerdy, gloriously specific. The whole tower of Babel under one low ceiling.

hook: The tallest covered passage, a cathedral of iron and glass
facts: Built 1825 near rue de Turbigo, between rue Saint-Denis and rue Dussoubs.
At nearly twelve metres it is the tallest of the Paris covered passages.
Its iron skeleton allowed two storeys of fully glazed shopfronts; restored in 1990.
w: Light pours down nearly twelve metres of iron and glass, the loftiest of the covered passages and built for craftsmen, not luxury. Saved and scrubbed up in 1990, it now shelters designers and curio sellers under that soaring roof. Step off the Montorgueil bustle into a sudden vertical hush. Workmanlike grandeur.

hook: A horseshoe of cottage gardens that defies the city grid
facts: A private pedestrian lane laid out in 1926 on the south flank of the Butte-aux-Cailles.
It loops in a tight horseshoe off rue du Moulin-des-Pres before dead-ending.
Lined with low houses, each with its own small garden; opened to the public in 1959.
w: A cobbled horseshoe of low houses and front gardens that curls off the grid and simply stops. Laid out in 1926 on the quiet back slope of the Butte-aux-Cailles, it feels like a village smuggled into the thirteenth. Wisteria, climbing roses, a cat or two. No through traffic, no reason to be here but to wander. Improbable calm.
